title: Wastewater AMR Surveillance: the Standardization Bottleneck
excerpt: Post-COVID, sewage surveillance for resistance is scaling fast. The block is not pipes or sequencers — it is incomparable lab methods. Different labs use different assays, gene targets, and pipelines. Without harmonized protocols and reference standards, results don't line up.

title: The AMR Surveillance Push Will Stall in the Sequencing Lab, Not the Clinic
excerpt: One Health plans want genomic AMR surveillance across livestock and meat. The hidden choke-point is sequencing-lab capacity, not new drugs. US NARMS now sequences 100% of poultry Salmonella and Campylobacter. An Indonesian pilot showed nanopore works but is hard to integrate.

title: Source Diffusion 20260629 06 Green Hydrogen Electrolyzer Feedwater
excerpt: Electrolytic hydrogen needs about 9–10 litres of ultrapure water per kilogram. A 1 GW electrolyzer draws ~200 m³/h — a midsize town's daily water. Feed-water quality is the single biggest maintenance variable in a stack.

title: Rapid Antibiotic Tests Work — Once the Hospital Is Wired to Use Them
excerpt: Hospitals bought fast antibiotic-susceptibility tests; resistance and mortality barely moved. The gate is not device speed — it is wiring results into stewardship. Pittsburgh cut time-to-right-antibiotic from 46 to 7 hours by integrating both.

title: Hospital Sewers Can Be Sequenced — They Just Can't Yet Be Trusted
excerpt: Hospitals are sequencing their sewers to spot superbugs early. But sewer signals have not been shown to predict ward infections. Stanford caught COVID trends in wastewater; MRSA and VRE did not track. The gate is validation evidence — no trusted threshold to act on.

title: Antibiotic Subscription Models Create a Reserved-Drug Access And Stewardship Data Bottleneck
excerpt: The UK pays a flat "Netflix" fee for reserve antibiotics — about £10m per drug a year. The fee guarantees supply, but the drugs stay tightly restricted. The real gate is aseptic IV compounding — not every hospital has it.

title: The PFAS Cleanup Clock Runs Through Carbon Kilns
excerpt: EPA set 4 ppt drinking-water limits for PFOA and PFOS. The 2026 proposal could move some PFOA/PFOS compliance to 2031. Calgon plans 27 million lb/year of added potable-carbon reactivation. OCWD procurement records show vessel and resin costs becoming separate line items.

title: The Battery Boom's Fire Gate Is Becoming a Documentation Market
excerpt: 24 GW | U.S. battery storage is planned for 2026, but that number is still a permitting outcome. 657 days | Potentia ran from initial filing to CEC approval, even while the post-completeness clock hit 270 days.

title: Offshore Wind's Hidden Gate Is the Maritime Construction Calendar
excerpt: The easy explanation for U.S. offshore wind's troubles is politics. Permits are challenged, federal agencies change direction, developers sue, and leases get cancelled or bought back.

title: Cancer's Next Supply Chain Is a Radioisotope Clock
excerpt: Novartis' two commercial RLT products reached $2.810B of 2025 net sales: Pluvicto at $1.994B and Lutathera at $816M. 200+ centers now order Pluvicto, ~130 more planned — after a 2023 manufacturing shortage.

title: The Hospital Capacity Bottleneck Hiding in a Bag of Salt Water
excerpt: Hospital capacity is usually described in visible terms: beds, nurses, operating rooms, ventilators, and reimbursement. Those are real constraints. But the 2024 IV-fluid shortage showed that a hospital can also be throttled by something far less dramatic: a validated bag of…

title: The Engine Shop Is the New Gate on Airline Capacity
excerpt: Airline capacity is usually discussed as an airframe problem. Boeing has quality and certification constraints. Airbus has a deep backlog and a slower-than-promised path to higher A320 output.

title: The Subsea Cable Bottleneck Is Who Can Repair the Break
excerpt: Submarine cables carry over 99% of international data; the risk is repair. The scarce asset is a ready cable ship, not more fiber. Two-thirds of repair ships reach end-of-life by 2040. Sustaining the fleet needs about $3 billion for roughly 20 ships.

title: The Shell Bottleneck Starts Before the Shell
excerpt: Western rearmament looks like a shell-body problem; the gate is chemistry. A complete 155mm shot needs fill, propellant, primer, and fuze. The US had to rebuild domestic TNT production after decades of none.

title: The Grid Bottleneck Is Whether the Model Survives the Fault
excerpt: A solar plant can pass every milestone and still fail at a fault. The new gate is proof the plant’s model matches real behavior. Disturbances since 2016 keep finding model-versus-reality mismatches. FERC Order 901 turned that gap into enforceable reliability standards.

title: The AI Power Trade Is Becoming a Turbine Slot Trade
excerpt: The standard story about AI power is easy to summarize: data centers are growing faster than the grid. That is true, but it is already becoming too broad to be useful. A hyperscaler can sign a power purchase agreement. A utility can announce new generation.

title: The AI Buildout’s Next Constraint Is the Air Permit
excerpt: AI campuses need power faster than the grid can deliver it. The workaround is on-site combustion — gated by an air permit. One behind-the-meter plant can emit roughly 190 tons of NOx a year. xAI’s Memphis turbines became a Clean Air Act fight, not an IT story.
